title: 〜とか〜とか
description: 〜とか〜とか lists examples in a casual, non-exhaustive way — "things like A and B." It implies there are other unlisted items too.

content.text: とか comes after each item in a list to give representative examples rather than a complete set — similar to や but more casual and conversational. It attaches to nouns and also to plain-form verbs and adjectives.

content.japanese: A ＋ とか ＋ B ＋ とか
content.english: things like A and B (and so on)

content.japanese: 週末（しゅうまつ）は掃除（そうじ）とか洗濯（せんたく）とかをします。
content.english: On weekends I do things like cleaning and laundry.

content.japanese: りんごとかバナナとかが好（す）きです。
content.english: I like things like apples and bananas.

content.japanese: 日本語（にほんご）とか韓国語（かんこくご）とかを勉強（べんきょう）しています。
content.english: I'm studying things like Japanese and Korean.

content.japanese: 映画（えいが）を見（み）るとか、本（ほん）を読（よ）むとかして過（す）ごした。
content.english: I spent the time doing things like watching movies and reading books.

content.text: Softening with a Single とか
A single とか can also soften a statement or introduce a vague quote, making it sound less direct — "something like ~" or "(they) said something about ~."

content.japanese: 疲（つか）れたとか言（い）って、来（こ）なかった。
content.english: He said something like he was tired and didn't come.

content.japanese: 明日（あした）は忙（いそが）しいとか。
content.english: Apparently they're busy tomorrow or something.

content.text: とか is casual — in formal writing or speech, use や (for nouns) or 〜たり〜たり (for verbs) instead. Also, unlike と (which lists an exhaustive set), とか always implies the list is just examples with more left unsaid.

examples.japanese: A ＋ とか ＋ B ＋ とか
examples.english: things like A and B (and so on)

examples.japanese: 週末（しゅうまつ）は掃除（そうじ）とか洗濯（せんたく）とかをします。
examples.english: On weekends I do things like cleaning and laundry.

examples.japanese: りんごとかバナナとかが好（す）きです。
examples.english: I like things like apples and bananas.

examples.japanese: 日本語（にほんご）とか韓国語（かんこくご）とかを勉強（べんきょう）しています。
examples.english: I'm studying things like Japanese and Korean.

examples.japanese: 映画（えいが）を見（み）るとか、本（ほん）を読（よ）むとかして過（す）ごした。
examples.english: I spent the time doing things like watching movies and reading books.

examples.japanese: 疲（つか）れたとか言（い）って、来（こ）なかった。
examples.english: He said something like he was tired and didn't come.

examples.japanese: 明日（あした）は忙（いそが）しいとか。
examples.english: Apparently they're busy tomorrow or something.

headings.text: Softening with a Single とか
